question: Define a function. What are the two main properties that a relation must satisfy to be considered a function? Provide a simple example and a counterexample.
expectedPoints: A function f from set A to set B assigns exactly one element of B to each element of A.
The two properties are: 1) Every element in the domain A must have an image in the codomain B (Totality). 2) No element in A is assigned more than one element in B (Uniqueness).
Example: f: {1,2} -> {a,b} defined by f(1)=a, f(2)=b. Counterexample: A relation where f(1)=a and also f(1)=b.

question: Explain the concept of a one-to-one (injective) function. Formally define it and then rephrase the definition in your own words. Provide one example of an injective function and one example of a function that is not injective, using small finite sets.
expectedPoints: Formal definition: A function f: A -> B is injective if for all a1, a2 in A, if f(a1) = f(a2) then a1 = a2.
Rephrased: Distinct inputs always map to distinct outputs; no two different elements in the domain map to the same element in the codomain.
Example (Injective): f: {1,2} -> {a,b,c} with f(1)=a, f(2)=b. Example (Not Injective): g: {1,2,3} -> {a,b} with g(1)=a, g(2)=a, g(3)=b.

question: Compare and contrast the properties of plain functions and one-to-one (injective) functions. Describe a real-world scenario that can be modeled by an injective function and explain why injectivity is a critical property in that context. Furthermore, prove that the function f: R -> R defined by f(x) = 2x + 3 is injective.
expectedPoints: Contrast: All injective functions are functions, but not all functions are injective. A plain function must assign exactly one output to each input, while an injective function has the additional constraint that outputs are unique to their inputs.

Real-world scenario: A national identification number system. Each citizen (domain) must be assigned a unique ID number (codomain). Injectivity is critical to ensure no two people share the same ID, preventing misidentification.
Proof: Assume f(a) = f(b). Then 2a + 3 = 2b + 3. Subtracting 3 from both sides gives 2a = 2b. Dividing by 2 yields a = b. Therefore, by definition, f is injective.
